candidates.name: Mike Dunleavy
candidates.office: Governor of Alaska (term-limited — second consecutive term ends Dec 7, 2026; not seeking reelection)
candidates.notes: Sitting AK Governor, term-limited (ends Dec 7, 2026), not seeking reelection (candidacy_status corrected from 'incumbent_seeking_reelection'). Documented record: pro-life ('most pro-life state' goal; Right to Life Day proclamations; vetoed abortion-related court funding [ruled unconstitutional, reversed]; vetoed a 2024 contraception-access measure); pro-women's-sports/anti-trans-in-girls'-sports; parental-rights bill (consent for sex-ed and name/pronoun changes); strong correspondence/homeschool & school-choice defender; signed porn age-verification (HB254); Day of Prayer + kept churches open during COVID; joined the 19-governor anti-ESG alliance; major budget/PFD spending vetoes; signed HB49 repealing SB91 (tough-on-crime); 'Statehood Defense' litigation vs. federal overreach (ANWR, vaccine mandates); authorized National Guard to the southern border; NRA 'A'-rated, signed HB61 (gun-store protection). DEVIATION: favors more legal immigration ('many doors').

profile.education: Misericordia University (BA History 1983), University of Alaska Fairbanks (MEd Education)
profile.background: Mike Dunleavy — Republican Governor of Alaska since December 2018 (re-elected 2022). Former state senator and educator. Term-limited under the Alaska Constitution (no third consecutive term); his term ends December 7, 2026 and he is not on the 2026 gubernatorial ballot.
profile.confidence_note: 2026-06-17 — Scored from documented official actions (bills signed/vetoed, proclamations, executive/board actions, multistate litigation, NRA rating). profile.background: Former U.S. Rep AK-At-Large (D), Sep 13 2022 – Jan 3 2025. Won 2022 special election (Aug 16 2022) over Sarah Palin via RCV — first Alaska Native ever elected to Congress, first woman to AK's at-large House seat. Lost 2024 re-election to Nick Begich III. ON JAN 12 2026 announced 2026 U.S. Senate campaign challenging incumbent Sen. Dan Sullivan (R). Former ED Kuskokwim River Inter-Tribal Fish Commission; AK state House 1999–2009 (Bethel). Russian Orthodox / Yup'ik. Father Ward Sattler (German-American pilot/teacher); mother Elizabeth 'LizAnn' Williams (Yup'ik). Husband Eugene 'Buzzy' Peltola Jr. died Sept 12 2023 in a Piper Super Cub crash near St. Mary's, AK; 7 children (blended).

claims.text: NARAL Pro-Choice America (now Reproductive Freedom for All) endorsed Peltola for her 2024 reelection, citing her commitment to 'locking protections for reproductive health care into federal law' — placing her inside the abortion-industry endorsement network the rubric identifies as disqualifying.

Earned the NRA's endorsement in her 2024 reelection campaign — making her the only Democrat in Congress to receive an NRA endorsement that cycle — citing her defense of Alaskans' right to own firearms for subsistence hunting and stating 'guns are a part of Alaska's culture and a core tool of a subsistence lifestyle,' opposing sweeping federal gun-control restrictions.

During her 2022–2024 House term, Peltola crossed party lines to vote for Republican-backed legislation to deport undocumented migrants who assault police officers, and voted to condemn the Biden administration's handling of the southern border — a harder-than-average Democratic stance on immigration enforcement.

candidates.notes: Former U.S. Representative AK-AL (2022-2025). Considering 2026 D U.S. Senate challenge to Dan Sullivan. First Alaska Native member of Congress.

profile.confidence_note: 2026-05-20 — 2026 AK U.S. Senate challenger. Archetype: establishment_d.

claims.text: Consistently supported abortion rights throughout her 2022–2024 congressional service; earned the endorsement of EMILY's List and stated support for federal abortion access — rejecting any personhood-from-conception standard.

Received an NRA rating of 'D' for supporting universal background checks, mandatory waiting periods for firearm purchases, and raising the minimum purchase age for certain firearms to 21 — positions opposing the rubric's defense of unrestricted Second Amendment rights.

Broke with the Democratic Party in 2024 by voting for border enforcement measures, one of 78 party-line-defying votes during her congressional service; her aisle-crossing votes were concentrated in border and energy policy.

profile.confidence_note: 2026-05-20 — 2026 AK Governor race. Archetype: establishment_r. · 2026-05-20 — Deduped batch-suffix record (merged duplicate).

claims.text: Stated as a 2026 Alaska gubernatorial candidate that she would work with the Legislature to place a constitutional amendment before voters clarifying that nothing in the Alaska Constitution creates a right to abortion — affirming a pro-life posture aligned with the rubric's life-at-conception standard.

Longtime member of the National Rifle Association and the Chugiak-Eagle River Friends of the NRA; endorsed by President Donald Trump in her 2024 congressional run; her record reflects consistent opposition to new firearms restrictions — aligning with the rubric's defense of Second Amendment rights.

candidates.name: Bill Walker
candidates.office: Governor of Alaska (2026 I Candidate · former Gov 2014-2018 · independent)
candidates.notes: Former AK Governor (2014-2018, Independent). 2022 I gubernatorial nominee (lost). Considering 2026 comeback bid.

profile.confidence_note: 2026-05-20 — 2026 AK Governor race. Archetype: establishment_r.

claims.text: Co-authored a July 2022 op-ed pledging to 'rebuild Alaska, not take away reproductive rights,' committing to protect abortion access under Alaska's constitutional privacy clause and to veto any legislation restricting a woman's right to an abortion — a direct rejection of the life-at-conception standard.

As Alaska governor (2015-2018), proposed a new Alaska state income tax to address the state budget deficit — introducing a new tax burden on residents and businesses rather than cutting spending, contrary to the rubric's anti-deficit, no-new-taxes posture.

Signed Alaska's Medicaid expansion under the Affordable Care Act as governor in 2015, extending a major federal healthcare program into the state rather than exercising the option to opt out and limit federal overreach.
